channel.title: Defense Segment – AI Planning Commentary
channel.description: XGBoost SHAP-based macroeconomic narrative for US Defense Segment B3 Planning | Generated by Claude Sonnet

item.title: Defense Market Volume & Outlook — 2026 Forecast
item.description:
## Section 1 — Defense Market Volume & Outlook
**What FDEFX measures and why it matters.** FDEFX (Federal Defense Consumption Expenditures and Gross Investment) tracks the actual pace at which the US federal government is disbursing funds to defense suppliers — money that has moved, not money that has been promised. For a company entering the US defense segment in 2026 with no prior defense revenue, this is the most direct macro anchor available: it measures the size of the spending pool that defense suppliers collectively draw from. FDEFX is used here — rather than new defense orders — because it measures realised expenditures, which is the invoice-equivalent quantity and therefore aggregates consistently with the order-backlog and pipeline logic of B1 and B2 in the SAC planning model.
**Where spending stands today.** The last confirmed reading (Q4 2025) was 1,159.2 USD bn at an annualised spending rate — near the top of the 2020–2025 range of 872.0 to 1,161.9 USD bn. Year-on-year growth into that quarter was +3.2% (vs. Q4 2024's 1,122.7 USD bn). This is the base from which the 2026 forecast starts.
**The forecast trajectory.** The model projects a step-down from the Q4 2025 level of 1,159.2 USD bn to a 2026 average annualised spending rate of 901.1 USD bn — a gap of 258.1 USD bn. The January 2026 forecast opens at 906.6 USD bn, slightly higher than subsequent months, because the model's inputs at that point still include the most recent actual FDEFX readings, which were near 1,159 USD bn. As the forecast rolls forward month by month, those recent high readings cycle out of the model's input window and are replaced by the model's own (lower) prior predictions. By mid-year the forecast stabilises in a narrow band of 897.8 to 906.6 USD bn, where it effectively plateaus through December 2026.
**Placing 901.1 USD bn in context.** The 2026 forecast average of 901.1 USD bn sits in the lower-middle portion of the 2020–2025 historical range (872.0–1,161.9 USD bn). It is well above the floor of that range but roughly 260 USD bn below the most recent actual level. The training data for the model — spanning 2002 to 2025 — had a reference level of 733.5 USD bn, indicating that the bulk of historical observations over that longer period were considerably lower than current spending. That figure is cited only as historical context; it is not a target the model is pulling toward, and it should not be used as a planning floor.
---
item.dc:creator: claude-sonnet@anthropic.com

item.title: What Drives the B3 Forecast — SHAP Feature Interpretation
item.description:
## Section 2 — What Drives the Forecast
**The model is, in plain terms, a persistence model.** The three largest forecast drivers are all prior FDEFX spending levels — the values from one, two, and three months before each forecast date — contributing a combined 122.46 USD bn above the model's reference level. Every other feature in the top ten is also derived from FDEFX's own history (older lags and rolling averages), except for two external signals: the defense production index (IPB52300S) and lagged new defense orders (ADEFNO). Those two external signals together contribute just 6.42 USD bn to the forecast. The model does not meaningfully read policy signals from order flow or production data — it extrapolates recent spending.
**What this means for the controller — be direct about it.** Four practical consequences follow. First, in a stable spending environment, the model's extrapolation logic is a reasonable short-horizon tool: if the federal government continues disbursing at roughly recent rates, the forecast will track reality adequately. Second, the model will be slow to capture turning points driven by budget decisions, supplemental appropriations, or large contract awards that have not yet converted into actual outlays — those events simply are not what the model responds to. Third, and most concretely: the year-end order book (ADEFNO December 2025 at 18,774 USD mn, which is 19.0% above the 2025 full-year average of 15,780 USD mn) is a meaningful forward signal that the model largely ignores — it contributes only 0.86 USD bn to the forecast. The controller should treat that elevated order reading as a **qualitative upside risk that sits outside the model's quantitative output** and flag it explicitly in the SAC planning commentary. Fourth, the defense production index rose steadily through 2025 (109.48 in January to 112.96 in December) and contributes 5.56 USD bn — a mild positive, but small relative to the persistence drivers. Do not weight it heavily in any narrative built around this forecast.
---
item.dc:creator: claude-sonnet@anthropic.com

item.title: Quarterly Monitoring KPIs — Defense Segment
item.description:
## Section 3 — Quarterly Monitoring KPIs | # | KPI & Plain-Language Description | Official Source & URL | Update Cadence | What to Watch For | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **FDEFX — Federal Defense Consumption Expenditures & Gross Investment**
The actual annualised pace of US defense disbursements; the same series the forecast targets | FRED, US Federal Reserve / BEA: [https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/FDEFX](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/FDEFX) | Quarterly | Any sustained divergence between actual quarterly readings and the 897–907 USD bn forecast band signals the model needs recalibration | | 2 | **ADEFNO — New Defense Orders (Capital Goods)**
Orders placed by the US military with manufacturers; a leading signal for future expenditure flow | FRED / US Census: [https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/ADEFNO](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/ADEFNO) | Monthly | A persistent move above or below the Dec 2025 level of 18,774 USD mn would indicate the frozen-input assumption is no longer valid | | 3 | **IPB52300S — Defense & Space Industrial Production Index**
Measures how much defense equipment US manufacturers are currently producing relative to a 2017 baseline | FRED / Federal Reserve: [https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/IPB52300S](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/IPB52300S) | Monthly | A meaningful drop from the Dec 2025 level of 112.96 — signalling a production slowdown — would be a downside risk the model cannot self-correct for | | 4 | **DoD Comptroller — Defense Budget & Appropriations Status**
Tracks whether the DoD is operating under a full-year appropriation or a continuing resolution, which directly constrains spending authority | DoD Comptroller: [https://comptroller.defense.gov](https://comptroller.defense.gov) | Quarterly (or upon any budget event) | Transition to a continuing resolution or a debt-ceiling impasse would cap new spending commitments and create downside risk to the expenditure rate | ---
item.dc:creator: claude-sonnet@anthropic.com

item.title: Risks & Forecast Limitations — B3 Defense
item.description:
## Section 4 — Risks and Forecast Limitations
### (a) US Federal Budget Risk
The forecast assumes that the US federal government maintains spending authority consistent with recent appropriations. In practice, two budget mechanisms can interrupt this. A **continuing resolution (CR)** occurs when Congress does not pass a full-year defense appropriation by the start of the fiscal year (October 1); under a CR, the DoD is generally limited to spending at the prior year's rate and cannot start new programs or accelerate existing contracts. A **debt-ceiling impasse** — where Congress does not raise the statutory borrowing limit — can force broader federal spending constraints that affect defense outlays even if appropriations have passed. Either scenario would exert downward pressure on actual FDEFX readings relative to the forecast.
For current appropriations status, budget projections, and baseline defense spending levels, refer directly to the DoD Comptroller ([https://comptroller.defense.gov](https://comptroller.defense.gov)) and the Congressional Budget Office ([https://www.cbo.gov](https://www.cbo.gov)). No probability estimate is offered here; the controller should monitor those sources directly each quarter and flag any CR extension or debt-ceiling action as a downside scenario trigger in SAC.
### (b) Frozen External Inputs Limitation
The forecast holds ADEFNO and IPB52300S fixed at their December 2025 values for all twelve months of 2026. Only the FDEFX-based inputs update — the model feeds its own prior predictions back in as each new month is forecast. This means the external signals provide a fixed backdrop, not a dynamic one.
**The practical implication is this:** the forecast is not progressively less reliable as the year goes on — it is *event-sensitive*. Reliability breaks down at any point in the year where reality diverges materially from the December 2025 snapshot of those two inputs. A major new contract award that sharply lifts new defense orders above 18,774 USD mn, a supplemental appropriation that accelerates disbursements, or a production disruption that causes the industrial production index to fall materially from 112.96 — any of these events, whether they occur in February or October, would mean the frozen inputs are no longer a reasonable stand-in for current conditions. When any such event occurs, the controller should treat the B3 market volume figure as stale until the model is re-run with updated inputs. The qualitative signal to watch is straightforward: if ADEFNO or the production index moves materially in either direction from its December 2025 level, the forecast numbers in this analysis should not be used without revision.
---
item.dc:creator: claude-sonnet@anthropic.com

item.title: Executive Summary — US Defense Market 2026
item.description:
## Executive Summary
- **The 2026 US defense spending forecast averages 901 USD bn annualised — roughly 258 USD bn below the Q4 2025 actual rate of 1,159 USD bn.** - **The model extrapolates recent spending history; external signals add little. Confidence is reasonable in stable policy conditions, low near budget inflection points.** - **Most important KPI: actual FDEFX quarterly releases (FRED, quarterly cadence) — track against the 897–907 USD bn forecast band.** - **Primary budget risk: a continuing resolution or debt-ceiling impasse capping DoD spending authority below current appropriation levels.** - **Forecast is invalidated by events, not time: a major contract award, supplemental appropriation, or production disruption breaks the frozen-input assumption immediately.** - **Use a scenario range around 901 USD bn for B3 planning — a single point estimate overstates model precision here.**
item.dc:creator: claude-sonnet@anthropic.com
